name: Lawtey Highland Mine
short_description: Historic phosphate mining area in north-central Florida offering opportunities to search for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in disturbed mining ground. Flat terrain and year-round access make...
description:
The Lawtey Highland Mine is a former phosphate mining operation in Bradford County, Florida, situated in the gently rolling landscape of the state's phosphate belt. This region has been heavily mined for phosphate rock since the early 1900s, making it a productive area for mineral collectors seeking quartz crystals, feldspar fragments, and mica specimens exposed in overburden piles and excavated areas. The flat terrain at 199 feet elevation makes the site accessible for rockhounders of varying mobility levels.

The geology here reflects Florida's unique position within the Peninsular Florida Platform, where Cenozoic-age phosphorite deposits formed in ancient marine environments. Mining operations have extensively disturbed the landscape, exposing fresh mineral material that would otherwise remain buried. Collectors often find small to medium quartz clusters, feldspar phenocrysts, and mica flakes in the spoil heaps and worked areas.

Access to Lawtey Highland Mine requires contacting current property owners or operators, as much of the historic mining land is now private or under corporate management. Some areas may have limited public access depending on active mining operations and property boundaries. Always confirm access before visiting and respect all posted signage and barriers.

The flat topography and lack of significant elevation change mean this site is suitable for families and less experienced collectors. However, the history of industrial mining means the ground may be uneven, with holes and exposed mineral concentrations requiring careful footing. Bring sturdy shoes, a hand lens, and small specimen bags to maximize your collecting success.
history: The Lawtey Highland Mine area sits within Florida's Central Phosphate District, where Miocene and Pliocene phosphatic sediments accumulated in shallow marine basins millions of years ago. Commercial phosphate mining in this region began in the early 1900s and transformed the landscape through extensive strip mining operations that continue today, exposing fresh mineral assemblages from the deep overburden.
tips: Search overburden piles and freshly exposed banks for quartz crystal clusters; look for translucent to milky-white crystals ranging from thumbnail to fist-sized specimens. Use a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from consolidated matrix material, and always wear safety glasses when breaking rock. Focus on areas where different colored soil layers meet, as these contacts often concentrate mineral-rich material. Check after heavy rains when erosion exposes new specimens in accessible areas.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 199 feet elevation with minimal slope. Walking distance from parking varies by site access point, typically under one mile to productive collecting areas. Soil is predominantly sandy and clayey with disturbed ground from mining activity. Footing can be uneven on overburden piles; wear sturdy hiking boots. Some areas may have standing water in abandoned pits during wet seasons.
best_season: October - April. Florida's subtropical climate means summer months bring intense heat and frequent afternoon thunderstorms that create hazardous conditions in and around old mining pits. Fall through spring offers cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and more predictable weather for sustained rockhounding.
written_directions: From Starke, take Florida State Road 225 north toward Lawtey for approximately 8 miles. The Lawtey Highland Mine area lies east of SR 225 in the agricultural and mining landscape of central Bradford County. Use the GPS coordinates (30.05303, -82.051278) to navigate to the general location. Access roads to the actual mining site may be unpaved or restricted; confirm property access before attempting to reach the coordinates.
road_conditions: County roads are generally paved; local access roads may be unpaved and subject to mining operations. Confirm current conditions and access permissions before visiting.
parking_notes: Parking availability depends on current property access and mining operations. When accessible, informal pullouts along county roads near the site may provide parking for a few vehicles.
permit_link: Contact Bradford County Property Appraiser or private property owners for access authorization
collection_rules: This is private land. You must obtain explicit written permission from the property owner or mining operator before collecting. No commercial collection is permitted. Respect all posted signs, barriers, and no-trespassing notices. Mining operations may be active; stay clear of equipment and excavation areas. Collect only the mineral specimens you can safely carry.
rules:
Obtain property owner permission before entering
No collecting without explicit authorization
Stay clear of active mining equipment
Respect all posted signage and barriers
No commercial collection
Remove only what you can safely carry
Fill holes and restore ground when possible
nearest_services: Starke, approximately 8 miles south via SR 225. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with minimal elevation gain makes this site accessible to most hikers and those with limited mobility. Unpaved local roads and uneven overburden piles may present challenges. No developed trails; walking is off-trail across disturbed mining ground. Sturdy footwear is required. Some areas may have deep holes and standing water from abandoned pits.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Lawtey Highland Mine in Bradford County, Florida?
faq.answer: Lawtey Highland Mine offers quartz crystals, feldspar fragments, and mica specimens that have been exposed through historic phosphate mining operations. The quartz here ranges from clear to milky-white and is frequently found in small clusters. Feldspar phenocrysts appear as colorful fragments in the overburden material.

faq.question: How do I get permission to rockhound at Lawtey Highland Mine near Starke?
faq.answer: This is private property managed by mining operators and property owners. You must contact the Bradford County Property Appraiser's office or identify the current property owner to request written permission before collecting. Access is not guaranteed and may depend on active mining operations.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to search for minerals at Lawtey Highland Mine?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Florida's summer heat combined with afternoon thunderstorms and frequent rain make the wetter months dangerous around old mining pits and abandoned excavations. Fall and spring offer cooler temperatures and more stable ground conditions for safe rockhounding.

faq.question: Is Lawtey Highland Mine beginner-friendly for family rockhounding in Florida?
faq.answer: Yes, the flat terrain and lack of significant elevation change make this site suitable for families and inexperienced collectors once access permission is secured. The main challenges are uneven overburden piles and potential standing water in abandoned pits, so sturdy shoes and caution are essential. The site is easiest after dry periods when ground footing is most stable.

faq.question: What tools and safety gear do I need for rockhounding at Lawtey Highland Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer and chisel for specimen extraction, a hand lens for examining minerals, safety glasses to protect from flying rock fragments, and durable specimen bags. Wear sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support for navigating uneven mining ground. Bring sunscreen and plenty of water, as the flat landscape offers little shade.

faq.question: Are there other rockhounding sites near Lawtey Highland Mine in north-central Florida?
faq.answer: The Bradford County area and surrounding Alachua County have additional phosphate mining areas and mineral-bearing locations. Starke, approximately 8 miles south, is your closest hub for services and information about other public and private rockhounding opportunities in Florida's phosphate belt.
